title: Minimal action shortcut to adiabaticity in a driven Kitaev chain: competing gaps in a topological transition at finite-time
authors: Rafael Bentes de Sales, Krissia Zawadzki
abstract: One of the main difficulties in preparing many-body ground states is achieving the target state through simple counterdiabatic controls. For critical systems crossing a transition to a topological phase, this task becomes even more challenging due to the closing of the gaps in multiple symmetry sectors. This is the case of the Kitaev chain, whose transition between the trivial and topological phases involves states belonging to different symmetry sectors. In this work, we apply the recently introduced minimal action shortcut to adiabaticity (MA-STA) to a Kitaev chain and propose a multi-step strategy to obtain the optimal control protocol to drive the system across its different phases. Our results show that high fidelities can be achieved through the adapted MA-STA at time scales much shorter than those of linear ramp protocols. We also compare the performance of both controls in suppressing work fluctuations. These findings may guide the design of STA protocols in many-body systems where competing energy scales and symmetries shape the global dynamics.
summary: This paper addresses the challenge of driving a Kitaev chain through a topological phase transition without inducing excitations. The authors find that standard shortcuts to adiabaticity fail when multiple momentum-dependent gaps close simultaneously. By proposing a multi-step 'two-plateau' optimization strategy, they demonstrate that high-fidelity state preparation can be achieved much faster than traditional linear ramps. The study also highlights how accounting for different momentum subspaces is crucial for effective control in many-body systems.
main_problem: The difficulty of preparing many-body ground states via shortcuts to adiabaticity (STA) in critical systems where multiple energy gaps close during a topological phase transition.
main_result: A multi-step 'two-plateau' Minimal Action Shortcut to Adiabaticity (MA-STA) protocol is proposed, which significantly outperforms linear ramps in achieving high fidelity and suppressing work fluctuations.
method: The authors use the Minimal Action Shortcut to Adiabaticity (MA-STA) framework to minimize an adiabatic action integral, employing a multi-step optimization strategy to handle competing momentum-dependent gaps.
model_or_system: A 1D Kitaev chain of spinless fermions with time-dependent chemical potential, characterized by hopping amplitude, superconducting pairing, and parity-conserving dynamics.
key_observables: State fidelity, work statistics (probability distribution, mean, variance, and skewness), and spectral gaps in different momentum sectors.
important_parameters: Chemical potential (driving parameter), superconducting gap (pairing amplitude), hopping amplitude, and the ratio of pairing to hopping (|delta|/omega).
main_assumptions: The use of the thermodynamic limit (bulk approximation) and the assumption that the Hamiltonian remains block-diagonal in parity sectors.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows energy eigenvalue evolution and spectral gaps; Figure 3 compares fidelity across different system sizes; Figure 4 illustrates the necessity of the two-plateau approach; Figure 7 displays work distribution and its moments.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of gap closures in topological transitions, defines the Kitaev chain model, presents the MA-STA optimization framework, analyzes the impact of competing gaps, proposes a multi-step strategy, and validates the results through fidelity and work statistics comparisons.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant for many-body dynamics as it provides a practical control strategy for navigating complex phase diagrams where multiple energy scales and symmetries compete, a common challenge in driving strongly correlated systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Evolution of the energy eigenvalues ε± 0 (purple) and ε± π (yellow) across the phase diagram. (b) Schematic of the minimal action protocols from µ0 < −2ω to µ f > 2ω, crossing the entire topological phase. (c) Spectral gaps Γk/ω versus the chemical potential µ/ω for |∆| = 2ω (top), |∆| = 0.5ω (middle), and |∆| = 0.1ω (bottom). In (a) and (c), the spectral gap closes at µ = −2ω for the k = 0 subspace (purple solid lines) and at µ = 2ω for the k = π subspace (yellow solid lines), defining the boundaries between the trivial and topological phases of the Kitaev chain. In (c), the curve with varying color represents ΓS 3, an energy gap solution that exists only when Eq. (11) is...

FIG. 2. Kitaev Chain’s phases. The main feature of the topological phase is the offset in the pairing of Majorana modes, which leaves MZMs unpaired at the ends of the chain. No unpaired quasi-particle is left in the trivial phase.

FIG. 3. Comparison between the two-plateaus protocol (2pMA, solid lines) and the linear ramp (LR, dashed lines) for |∆| = ω, driven between two trivial phases and crossing two transitions with µ0 = −µf = −3ω. System sizes N = 20, 50 and 80 are shown in red, green and blue, respectively. The minimal action protocol demonstrates superior performance, achieving high fidelity in significantly shorter time scales compared to the linear ramp across all considered system sizes.

FIG. 4. Fidelities (a) and time-dependent control protocol µ(t)/ω (b) for a Kitaev chain with N = 30 and |∆| = ω. The quench is performed from µ0 = 3ω to µ f = −3ω. Panel (a) shows that the MA-STA proto- col offers a significant advantage over the standard linear ramp (LR, blue dashed lines) in achieving high-fidelity state preparation at short time scales. However, within this framework, the two-plateau opti- mization (2pMA, solid red lines, illustrated on the right of Fig. 1b) is essential, as the single-plateau protocol (MA, dot-dashed black lines, left of Fig. 1b) is insufficient and fails to converge to the target state even for longer durations. This highlights that the additional...

FIG. 5. Fidelities for |∆| = ω, N = 60, and a quench from µ0 = 0 to µf = −3ω. The plots compare the MA-STA protocol (solid lines) with the linear ramp (dashed lines) for the even (red) and odd (blue) ground states. In (a), the control is optimized for the lowest- energy momentum subspace (ktarget = π/N), while in (b), it targets the second-lowest energy subspace (ktarget = 2π/N). Notably, the odd ground state fidelity is inherently higher due to its trivial dy- namics in the odd sector. Furthermore, panel (b) reveals an unex- pected improvement in the even ground state fidelity when targeting the second-lowest subspace, suggesting that optimal many-body con- trol requires accounting for...

FIG. 6. Fidelities for |∆| = ω, N = 14, and a quench from µ0 = 0 to µ f = −3ω. The plots compare the MA-STA protocol (solid lines) with the linear ramp (dashed lines) for the even (red) and odd (blue) ground states. In (a), the fidelity is calculated using the bulk approx- imation, while in (b), results are obtained via exact diagonalization. Although the bulk model predicts a clear advantage for the MA-STA protocol, the exact dynamics in (b) reveal that the linear ramp can perform better for small system sizes. These quantitative discrepan- cies arise because the bulk approximation neglects the discrete nature of the energy spectrum and finite-size effects, which are dominant at N = 14.

FIG. 7. Work probability distribution P(W) for the MA-STA protocol in (a) and (b), and distribution moments ((c), (d) and (e)) as a function of the quench duration ωτ. Results are obtained via exact diagonalization of a Kitaev spin chain with N = 14 sites and a quench from µ0 = 0 to µf = −3ω. Panels (a) and (b) show the evolution of the work distribution starting from the even and odd ground states, respectively. Panels (c), (d) and (e) show, respectively, the first, second and third moments of the distributions using the MA protocol (solid lines) and the linear ramp (dashed lines) for both the even (red) and odd (blue) ground states. Insets in (c)-(e) highlight the sudden quench regime (ωτ...

FIG. 8. Fidelity for various values of ∆with µ0 = −3ω, µ f = 0 and N = 60. The total time goes up to ωτ = 120 (a), and at ωτ = 60 (gray plane) the fidelities are plotted for continuously varying ∆(b). Results’ colors change from blue for ∆= 0.1ω, to black for ∆= 0.5ω and to red for ∆= ω, with solid lines for the MA-STA protocol and dashed for the simple linear ramp.
